Atomistic Simulation of HF Etching Process of Amorphous  $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  Using Machine Learning Potential
Changho Hong, $^{\parallel}$  Sangmin Oh, $^{\parallel}$  Hyungmin An, Purun-hanul Kim, Yaeji Kim, Jae-hyeon Ko, Jiwoong Sue, Dongyean Oh, Sungkye Park, and Seungwu Han*

ABSTRACT: An atomistic understanding of dry-etching processes with reactive molecules is crucial for achieving geometric integrity in highly scaled semiconductor devices. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are instrumental, but the lack of reliable force fields hinders the widespread use of MD in etching simulations. In this work, we develop an accurate neural network potential (NNP) for simulating the etching process of amorphous  $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  with HF molecules. The surface reactions in diverse local environments are considered by incorporating several types of training sets: baseline structures, reaction-specific data, and general-purpose training sets. Furthermore, the NNP is refined through iterative comparisons with the density functional theory results. Using the trained NNP, we carry out etching simulations, which allow for detailed observation and analysis of key processes such as preferential sputtering, surface modification, etching yield, threshold energy, and the distribution of etching products. Additionally, we develop a simple continuum model, built from the MD simulation results, which effectively reproduces the surface composition obtained with MD simulations. By establishing a computational framework for atomistic etching simulation and scale bridging, this work will pave the way for more accurate and efficient design of etching processes in the semiconductor industry, enhancing device performance and manufacturing precision.
KEYWORDS: machine learning potential, plasma etching, molecular dynamics, density functional theory, multiscale simulation, silicon nitride
1. INTRODUCTION

Modern semiconductor devices consist of complex three-dimensional structures with critical dimensions spanning only a few nanometers. An example is the high-density flash memory used in many electronic devices. To increase storage density, multilayer deep vertical structures are fabricated with high aspect ratios of  $\sim 100$  and hole radii of  $100\mathrm{nm}$ . Achieving geometric integrity of such highly anisotropic structures requires sophisticated dry-etching processes using plasma gases. In optimizing the process conditions and improving yields, the computational techniques have been instrumental, playing a pivotal role in advancing process technology. For instance, the composition and spatial distribution of plasma gases are simulated using computational fluid dynamics (CFD) coupled with chemical kinetics and equilibrium theory. The
simulation results are useful in maintaining the structural integrity across the wafer, for instance. $^{10}$

As flash memory is further scaled by increasing the number of stacking layers and reducing the pitch dimension, the demand for accurate prediction of the etch profile is increasing to minimize shape distortions. This, in turn, necessitates a simulation module for describing plasma-surface interactions. A popular approach is the plasma-chemistry-based Monte

Carlo method. $^{12}$  In this mesoscale simulation, the material is coarse-grained into voxels with side lengths of  $5 - 10\mathrm{nm}$ , and possible chemical interactions are defined with reaction rates. For each incident plasma particle, reactions such as etching, deposition, and passivation are determined probabilistically by the Monte Carlo method, and the state of the voxels evolves accordingly. (It is also worth noting a development using the level set method. $^{13}$ ) In tandem with the CFD simulations yielding distributions for ions and neutrals near surface, the Monte Carlo method selects reactions and products based on the composition of the voxel and the incident particle, thereby emulating etch profiles depending on the process conditions. However, the model includes many parameters for surface reactions, most of which are difficult to determine precisely from the known chemistry. The experimental information is not sufficiently detailed to circumvent this problem, either. Furthermore, as new etching gases are introduced, the phenomenological fitting of simulation parameters is increasingly difficult. $^{8}$

The foregoing discussions highlight the limitation of current computational framework in dry-etching simulation with plasma, that mainly originates in the lack of detailed atomistic information on the plasma-surface interaction. This can be augmented by bottom-up approaches based on molecular dynamics (MD) simulations that directly emulate atomic processes in ion etching. By following the atomic trajectory using Newton's law of motion, the MD simulation can enlighten the etching mechanism and offer various parameters such as etching yield without resorting to empirical information. For instance, physical sputtering by inert gas and reactive ion etching (RIE) has been studied using classical potentials or reactive force-fields (ReaxFFs). From the simulation, useful information such as etch rate was obtained together with fundamental understanding on the etching process. However, the available potential model is highly limited, and parametrization requires human expert knowledge and a long period of development. As an alternative, the density functional theory (DFT) is a robust and accurate approach with little empiricism. However, performing MD for etching simulations using DFT, which spans multiple nanoseconds with at least thousands of atoms, is intractable.

Recently, machine learning potentials (MLPs) have gained significant attention as they can evaluate energies with accuracy comparable to DFT, yet at a significantly lower cost.[20] This efficiency enabled the accelerated prediction of crystal structures,[21] phase diagrams,[22] and thermal and diffusional properties in bulk materials.[23,24] MLPs have also proven to be useful in various surface simulations, such as catalysis at surfaces,[25] physical sputtering simulations,[26] and surface oxidation processes.[27] While the use of MLPs in atomistic etching simulations seems promising, preparing the DFT training data presents significant challenges.[28] This is because sampling chemical reactions for these simulations requires long-term MD simulations, which are costly with DFT. Furthermore, it is essential to accurately capture the diverse environmental conditions encountered during simulations.

Motivated by foregoing discussions, in this study, we suggest a protocol that can produce reliable MLPs for surface reactions, which can be used in atomistic etching simulation. This protocol consists of several types of training set; baseline structures, reaction-specific data, together with general-purpose training sets. Furthermore, the MLP is refined through iterative comparisons with DFT results. As a showcase example, we

simulate the RIE of amorphous  $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  (a- $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$ ) by HF molecules using MD simulations with a neural network potential (NNP). In industry, hydrofluorocarbons are more favored when dry etching silicon nitride,[29] but for simplicity we focus on HF gas in this work. To examine the kinetic effect of reactive ions, HF molecules are incident with kinetic energy of  $10 - 50\mathrm{eV}$ , which is the typical energy of ions colliding with the surface without bias.[30] We conduct MD simulations of a- $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  being etched by HF molecules and analyze etching characteristics, including the etching yield and surface profile, with respect to the energy and angle of incidence. Furthermore, we develop a simple mesoscale model, built from our MD simulation results, which effectively reproduces the surface composition obtained with MD simulations.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows: in Section 2, we explain the types of NNP employed in this work and training method (Section 2.1), DFT parameters for generating the training data (Section 2.2), and the simulation setup for etching process (Section 2.3). In Section 3.1, we focus on the training of the NNP, including data set construction and performance evaluation. In Section 3.2, we examine the etching process under various incident energies and angles using the trained NNP. The microscale simulation results are connected to a mesoscale model in Section 3.3. Finally, we summarize and conclude this work in Section 4.
2. COMPUTATIONAL METHODS

2.1. Hybrid Neural Network Potential. During RIE, atoms experience close interactions due to high-energy impacts. Sampling these configurations using DFT would require significant computational resources. As an alternative, these close atomic interactions can be effectively modeled with the screened Coulomb potential, which integrates well with MLPs.[31-33] Following prior studies, we employ the pairwise Ziegler-Biersack-Littmark  $(\mathrm{ZBL})^{34}$  potential to simulate the interactions between energetic atoms. Thus, the potential energy  $(E_{\mathrm{pot}})$  is decomposed as follows

where  $E_{\mathrm{ZBL}}$  and  $E_{\mathrm{NNP}}$  represent the energy from ZBL potential and NNP, respectively. In eq 1,  $E_{\mathrm{ZBL}}$  is defined as the sum of pairwise repulsive potentials  $E_{ij}^{\mathrm{ZBL}}$

where  $N_{\mathrm{a}}$  is the number of atoms in a structure,  $\mathcal{N}(i)$  denotes the neighborhood of the ith atom,  $r_{ij}$  represents the interatomic distance,  $Z_{i}$  is the atomic charge,  $\epsilon_0$  is the vacuum permittivity,  $\Phi (r_{ij} / a)$  is the screening function in the ZBL potential with a screening length  $a$ , and  $S(r_{ij})$  is the switching function. The screening function  $\Phi (x)$  is expressed as  $0.18175\mathrm{e}^{-3.19980x} + 0.50986\mathrm{e}^{-0.94229x} + 0.28022\mathrm{e}^{-0.4029x} + 0.02817\mathrm{e}^{-0.20162x}$  and the screening length  $a$  is defined as  $0.4685 / (Z_i^{0.23} + Z_j^{0.23})$  in Å. $^{34}$  The switching function  $S(x)$  in eq 3 ensures a smooth decay of the ZBL potential to zero at finite distances, using a fourth-order polynomial (details are available in the LAMMPS documentation $^{35}$ ). This function introduces two cutoff distances: the inner cutoff  $(r_{\mathrm{c}}^{\mathrm{in}})$  and the outer cutoff  $(r_{\mathrm{c}}^{\mathrm{out}})$ . At  $r_{ij} = r_{\mathrm{c}}^{\mathrm{out}}$ ,  $S(x)$  cancels out the screened Coulomb potential such that  $E_{ij}^{\mathrm{ZBL}} = 0$ . For  $r_{ij} < r_{\mathrm{c}}^{\mathrm{in}}$ ,  $S(x)$  becomes a constant, contributing zero to the force. The parametrization of  $S(x)$  ensures that the ZBL potential is smooth up to the second derivative at  $r_{\mathrm{c}}^{\mathrm{in}}$  and  $r_{\mathrm{c}}^{\mathrm{out}}$ . We tune the cutoff radii for each pair of atomic species (Si, N, H, and F) as follows: the equilibrium distance  $(d_{\mathrm{eq}})$  is first obtained for each dimer in vacuum

To train hybrid-style NNP in eq 1, we utilize the residual energy, which is obtained by subtracting the ZBL potential from the DFT energy. The energy loss function  $(\Gamma_{E})$  in batch training is given as follows

where  $M$  is the number of structures in a batch,  $N_{i}$  is the number of atoms in the ith structure, and  $E_{\mathrm{DFT(ZBL,NNP)}}^{i}$  is the DFT (ZBL, NNP) energy of the ith structure. The loss functions for force and stress are defined similarly for each component.

We utilize SIMPLE-NN package to train the Behler-Parrinello-type NNP, which employs atom-centered symmetry functions (ACSFs) as the input features. The numbers of radial and angular features for both Si and N are 24 and 96, respectively, while corresponding numbers for both H and F are 16 and 60, respectively. The cutoff radius for ACSF is  $4.5\AA$  except for  $6\AA$  for the radial features between Si and N. Further details of ACSFs are compiled in Table S2. As a preprocessing, the descriptors are first scaled using the maximum and minimum values for each ACSF and then decorrelated using principal component analysis with whitening. The fully connected neural network has two hidden layers with 60 nodes each and uses a hyperbolic tangent activation function. The model is L2-regularized with a coefficient of  $10^{-6}$ . We split the structures of the data set into  $90\%$  for the training set and  $10\%$  for the test set. The training is carried out in two steps, each with 100 epochs, but differing in learning rate and minibatch size. The first step employs a learning rate of  $10^{-4}$  and a batch size of 4, while the subsequent step uses a learning rate of  $10^{-5}$  and a batch size of 24. For optimization, we utilize the Adam optimizer.

2.2. DFT Calculations. In constructing training data, we carry out DFT computations by using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP). The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional is used to approximate the exchange-correlation energy among electrons. To optimize computational efficiency, we initially perform ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations with a low-fidelity setup employing soft-core pseudopotentials for N and F and unpolarized

spin configurations. Subsequently, these trajectories are subsampled and recalculated with a high-fidelity setting, which incorporates standard pseudopotentials, a higher cutoff energy of  $500\mathrm{eV}$ , and spin-polarized functional. In the supercell calculations, only the  $\Gamma$  point is sampled for the Brillouin zone integration and the temperature is controlled by the Nosé-Hoover thermostat.[40] During the AIMD, the hydrogen mass is tripled to enable a larger time step of 1 fs, while ensuring the stability of the simulations at high temperatures. In cases where hydrogen is absent in the AIMD simulations, the time step is set to 2 fs.

2.3. MD Simulation for Etching. We conduct MD simulations of etching processes using an NNP within the LAMMPS software. $^{41}$  The etching simulation involves the repeated impact of HF molecules on a substrate in the  $xy$  plane. Each impact cycle introduces a randomly oriented HF molecule with an initial velocity  $(
u_{\mathrm{ini}})$ , starting from a position that is random along the lateral directions and at least 6 Å above the substrate surface. The azimuth angle of  $
u_{\mathrm{ini}}$  is chosen randomly, while the incident energy  $(E_{\mathrm{in}})$  and the angle of incidence  $(	heta_{\mathrm{in}})$  —defined as the angle between  $
u_{\mathrm{ini}}$  and the negative of the surface normal—are specifically selected for each simulation.

After each introduction of an HF molecule, the simulation proceeds in the NVE ensemble for 2 ps, followed by an NVT simulation at  $300\mathrm{K}$  using the Nose-Hoover thermostat. Once the temperature stabilizes at  $300\mathrm{K}$ , the next HF molecule is introduced. To prevent the substrate slab from drifting after impacts, the bottom layer with a thickness of  $2\AA$  is fixed.

During the bombardment by ions, high velocities or accelerations of atoms can introduce errors into the velocity Verlet algorithm. To mitigate these errors and save computational resources, we adopt a variable time step  $(\Delta t)$ . The value of  $\Delta t$  for each MD step is determined under three conditions: the atom displacement does not exceed  $0.1\AA$ , the kinetic energy transfer is limited to  $1\mathrm{eV}$ , and  $\Delta t$  does not exceed  $0.5\mathrm{fs}$ .

The van der Waals (vdW) interactions are crucial for interactions between the surface and adsorbed gas molecules, significantly affecting desorption rates of gas molecules that are weakly bound to the surface. Neglecting vdW interactions could lead to an inaccurate representation of molecular behaviors, such as exaggerated desorption rates. Since the reference DFT data sets are obtained within PBE, which poorly describes vdW interactions, the vdW contribution is separately added to  $E_{\mathrm{pot}}$ . Thus

3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

3.1. Training Neural Network Potential. The objective of this study is to investigate the etching process of  $\mathrm{a - Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  by HF molecules using MD simulations. To develop an NNP suitable for the simulation, constructing comprehensive training sets that accurately reflect the chemical reactions in the HF etching process is crucial. However, identifying all possible chemical reactions exhaustively is computationally infeasible. To address this challenge, we propose a strategy that involves constructing diverse training sets along with an iterative training for further refinements. (See Figure 1.). An overview of the training sets is presented in Table 1. In the below, we provide details in the training sets, iterative training, and further verification of NNP.

text: 3.1.1. Training Data Set. The training sets are divided into three categories: baseline, reaction-specific, and general-purpose. We detail each category in the below.

3.1.1.1. Baseline Structures. The baseline structures include a diverse range of elementary structures, such as radicals, molecules, and both crystalline and amorphous forms of bulk and slab  $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$ . To sample the local potential energy surface of these structures, AIMD simulations at various temperatures are carried out. In particular, AIMD at elevated temperatures is beneficial for ensuring the stability of the NNP.[28]

The molecular subset of our training set encompasses single atoms and every possible diatomic combination of Si, N, H, and F. It also incorporates polyatomic molecules such as  $\mathrm{NH}_x\mathrm{F}_y$  ( $0 \leq x, y \leq 3$ ;  $0 < x + y \leq 3$ ) and  $\mathrm{SiH}_x\mathrm{F}_y$  ( $0 \leq x, y \leq 4$ ;  $0 < x + y \leq 4$ ). This aims to cover a wide range of potential byproducts, spanning from stable molecules to metastable

radicals. The polyatomic molecules  $\left(\mathrm{NH}_x\mathrm{F}_y\right.$  and  $\left.\mathrm{SiH}_x\mathrm{F}_y\right)$  are sampled during MD at  $1000\mathrm{K}$  for 2 ps while the diatomic molecules are sampled at discrete bond lengths.

For crystalline structures,  $\alpha$  and  $\beta$  phases of  $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  are considered with various strains and NVT-MD simulations at  $300\mathrm{K}$  for 2 ps. To obtain surface-related training data, we construct an orthorhombic slab structure of  $\beta \text{-Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  with dimensions of  $7.6\times 13.3\times 18.7\AA^3$ , exposing the (0001) plane with Si-F and N-H terminations. The local vibrations are sampled at temperatures of 300 and  $2000\mathrm{K}$ , for durations of 2 ps each.

To consider primitive defects generated during etching, we introduce both vacancies and interstitials of Si and N into the slab separately. Vacancies are created in the subsurface layer, taking crystallographic equivalence into account, while interstitials are positioned at the centers of both large and small channels. The training data are obtained from MD simulations conducted at  $2000\mathrm{K}$ , with durations ranging from 4 to 8 ps, until the local structures reach a steady state following reconstruction. Additionally, the incoming HF molecules can traverse the channels along the  $c$ -axis in  $\beta-\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$ . To account for this, we sample 5 ps MD trajectories in which an HF molecule passes through both slab and bulk channels with microcanonical ensemble. The HF molecule is initially placed at the center of the channel cross section with a kinetic energy of  $10\mathrm{eV}$ .

The bulk liquid and amorphous  $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  are sampled from melt-quench trajectories. The melt-quench simulation is carried out as follows: 48 Si and  $64\mathrm{~N}$  atoms are randomly distributed in a cubic cell with the length of  $1.06\mathrm{~nm}$ . The resulting density of  $3.11\mathrm{~g/cm}^3$  corresponds to the experimental density of a- $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$ .[44] This cell is first premelted at  $4000\mathrm{~K}$  (this stage is not included in the training set) and then maintained in a liquid state at  $3000\mathrm{~K}$  for 20 ps. Subsequently, it is quenched to  $300\mathrm{~K}$  at a rate of  $-100\mathrm{~K/ps}$ , and the resulting amorphous structure is annealed at  $500\mathrm{~K}$  for 15 ps. We then randomly cleave the bulk amorphous  $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  to form a slab structure and the slab is melted by increasing the temperature from 500 to  $3000\mathrm{~K}$  at a rate of  $250\mathrm{~K/ps}$ . Various surface configurations are sampled at  $500\mathrm{~K}$  for 5 ps and 3000 K for 20 ps (the MD trajectory, where temperature increases to  $3000\mathrm{~K}$ , is not sampled).

During the simulation, unreacted HF molecules can accumulate and aggregate on the substrate, forming hydrogen bonds among HF molecules as in a liquid phase. To account for this, the HF liquid is simulated for 10 ps with NVT ensemble at 300 and  $500\mathrm{K}$  and a density of  $0.971~\mathrm{g / cm^3}$  (density at  $292.9\mathrm{K}$ ).<sup>45</sup>

3.1.1.2. Reaction-Specific Structures. In addition to the aforementioned bulk and slab configurations, the training set should also encompass potential chemical reactions. These are not easily sampled during conventional MD simulations as chemical reactions are rare events with significant activation barriers. Here we utilize a guided MD approach to effectively sample reaction pathways. This technique can accelerate and sample reactions within the MD time scale by employing constrained MD and gradually reducing the constraints until the desired configuration is attained.[46,47] We take advantage of the observation that H tends to bond with N, while F prefers to bond with Si. This bonding preference is supported by the experimental analysis on surface states following etching with HF solution.[48] Reflecting this, we conduct guided MD

3.1.1.3. General-Purpose Data Set. During collision cascades from high-energy impacts, a wide range of unstable configurations with low coordination numbers can be generated, which may not be sufficiently sampled in the baseline or reaction-specific data set obtained under relatively mild conditions. In addition, the high-temperature AIMD can help to increase accuracy in unexpected structures.[28] Therefore, we construct a general-purpose data set that samples configurations from high-temperature MD simulations of  $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  slab, which results in active bond breaking and formations. We

consider three types of slab structures: the first one is the cleaved crystalline slab structure, the same structure as in baseline structures. The other two are created by removing either two Si atoms or two N atoms from the top surface and passivating the dangling bonds around N and Si with H and F atoms, respectively. The variation in composition is implemented to sample different disordered structures. The bottom two layers of the slab are fixed, including the terminating atoms, so as to partially maintain the crystal structure at high temperatures. This approach would allow the sampling of approximate interfaces between highly disordered structures and the stoichiometric bulk during collisions. The sampling temperatures are 4500 and  $10,000\mathrm{K}$ , using the NVT ensemble for 10 ps. Figure S1 illustrates the structures from high-temperature MD at  $10,000\mathrm{K}$  which contain disordered surfaces of  $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  and diverse molecules. We note that the general-purpose training set is crucial because eliminating this set results in unstable MD simulations during etching process (results not shown).

With the above-mentioned training sets (3.1.1.1-3), we train a primitive NNP. The root-mean-square error (RMSE) for the test set is  $18 \, \mathrm{meV} / \mathrm{atom}$  for energy,  $0.64 \, \mathrm{eV} / \AA$  for force, and  $8.6 \, \mathrm{kB}$  for stress. (The parity plots are provided in Figure S2a-c). As a basic test, we compare structural properties of the  $\mathrm{a - Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  obtained by the NNP with those obtained using DFT in Figure S2d,e. It is observed that the NNP well reproduces the radial and angular distributions.

3.1.2. Iterative Training. To evaluate and refine the primitive NNP, we carry out small-scale etching simulations using the primitive NNP on crystalline and amorphous  $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  surface models and obtain the DFT data for subsampled configurations, which are used for retraining the NNP iteratively. For the crystalline slab, we employ the same structure as in the baseline. To generate an amorphous slab, we utilize a melt-quench-anneal protocol using the NNP. In detail, 42 Si and  $56\mathrm{~N}$  atoms are first randomly distributed in a cubic cell with the length of  $1\mathrm{~nm}$ . This is followed by premelting at  $5000\mathrm{~K}$  for  $20\mathrm{~ps}$  and melting at  $2800\mathrm{~K}$  for  $100\mathrm{~ps}$ . The system is then quenched to  $300\mathrm{~K}$  at a rate of  $-10\mathrm{~K/ps}$ , and annealed at  $500\mathrm{~K}$  for  $20\mathrm{~ps}$ . The amorphous structure is randomly cleaved, and the obtained slab is annealed at  $300\mathrm{~K}$  for  $5\mathrm{~ps}$ .

In the etching simulations, we follow the procedures outlined in Section 2.3 except that the vdW interaction is not included for consistency of the functional with the primary data set. Each HF molecule is assigned a random  $E_{\mathrm{in}}$  and  $\theta_{\mathrm{in}}$  within the ranges of  $10 - 50 \mathrm{eV}$  and  $0 - 75^{\circ}$ , respectively. After the last HF molecule impacts, DFT calculations are performed on trajectories selected within the initial 2000 steps after each incidence ( $\sim 1$  ps), which are densely sampled at intervals of 40 steps. Subsequent trajectories are sampled at a less frequent interval of 200 steps. Structures where the energy difference exceeds  $15 \mathrm{meV} / \mathrm{atom}$  between NNP and DFT are incorporated into the training sets, and the NNP is retrained. The update of NNP is iterated through three cycles each for the crystalline and amorphous slabs, with each cycle beginning anew from a clean slab. The first iteration includes 25 incidences of HF molecules, while each subsequent iteration involves up to 50 HF molecule incidences.

Figure 3a illustrates the NNP and DFT energies along the MD trajectory for the first cycle of iterative training (before any refinements), which consists of 1533 subsampled configurations. It is seen that the NNP energy closely matches the DFT energy throughout the trajectory. The RMSEs of

The subsequent reductions in energy correspond to the cooling by the thermostat. After 25 HF incidences, 2 Si and  $3\mathrm{N}$  atoms are etched away and the final configuration is shown in Figure 3b.

In subsequent iterations of both the crystalline and amorphous slabs, the trends in NNP and DFT energy are similar to those shown in Figure 3. The RMSE values for energy and force across the trajectories are within the ranges of  $10 - 17\mathrm{meV / atom}$  and  $0.59 - 0.72\mathrm{eV} / \mathring{\mathrm{A}}$  respectively. These values do not seemingly represent significant improvement in the accuracy of NNP, but the iterative training helps to increase the accuracy in reaction energies, which will be discussed in Section 3.1.3.

3.1.3. Test on Reaction Energies. In the above, the accuracy of the NNP was examined by comparing it with DFT results for the sampled snapshots during small-scale etching MD. However, comparison of the total energy or atomic forces in large supercells can be obscured by thermal vibrations that exist throughout the whole system. To more directly compare the energy landscape for specific chemical reactions during the etching process, we first identify stable configurations during the etching MD with the crystalline slabs in the previous subsection. These are defined as snapshots where the topology of the bond network is well maintained over 100 consecutive steps. Here, two atoms are assumed to be bonded if the interatomic distance is less than 1.3 times the sum of their covalent radii. We identify 1 to 5 distinct stable states for each incidence and relax them using NNP and DFT to remove thermal fluctuations. The vdW interactions are omitted here to validate the accuracy of the NNP against PBE as the reference data are based on PBE.

We define the reaction energy  $(E_{\mathrm{rxn}})$  as the energy difference between two consecutive and distinct stable states. The energies for gaseous reactants and products are also considered. In total, 212 reactions are identified and their  $E_{\mathrm{rxn}}$  values are compared between DFT  $(E_{\mathrm{rxn}}^{\mathrm{DFT}})$  and NNP  $(E_{\mathrm{rxn}}^{\mathrm{NNP}})$  in Figure 4. A good correlation between  $E_{\mathrm{rxn}}^{\mathrm{DFT}}$  and  $E_{\mathrm{rxn}}^{\mathrm{NNP}}$  is found with the coefficient of determination  $(R^{2})$  of 0.88. These reactions encompass various processes, including bond breaking and rearranging Si-N bonds in the substrate, the bond formations between incoming molecules and the substrate, and desorption of byproducts. The results suggest that the refined NNP accurately describes a wide range of chemical reactions. We note that the primitive NNP without

3.2. Etching Simulations. We apply the NNP trained through the previous subsection to simulating etching process in larger a-Si $_3$ N $_4$  models with a surface area of  $2 \times 2 \, \mathrm{nm}^2$ , which are generated by following the melt-quench-annealing protocol in Section 3.1.2. The pristine a-Si $_3$ N $_4$  slabs are used based on the assumption that the initial termination does not affect the etching behavior in steady state. To explicitly show this, we also test with amorphous slabs with hydrogen termination, and confirm similar results with those from the pristine model. (See Table S3 and Figure S3 for details.)

Total 800 HF molecules are incident sequentially (see Section 2.3), which is equivalent to an ion dose of  $200\mathrm{HF}/\mathrm{nm}^2$ , over 3–6 ns. We note that the average molecular flux  $(\sim 10^{10}\mathrm{HF / nm}^2\cdot \mathrm{s})$  is roughly 8 orders of magnitude higher than the typical experimental condition in plasma etching.[49] Nevertheless, the interaction time of ions is known to be on the order of picoseconds,[7] so important dynamics can be simulated within the MD time scale ( $\sim 5$  ps/incidence in this study). For example, a similar MD simulation produced etching yields of  $\mathrm{SiO}_2$  and SiN by  $\mathrm{CF}_3^+$  ions that agree well with experimental results.[50] However, long-term reactions such as densification or annealing may not be fully simulated with the current MD protocol, which might result in differences in etching yield and chemical composition at surfaces. Such effects are of interest for future study.

The simulations encompass a range of  $E_{\mathrm{in}}$  (from 10 to 50 eV) at normal incidence, as well as variations in  $\theta_{\mathrm{in}}$  (from 0 to  $75^{\circ}$ ) at a fixed energy of 50 eV. For each incidence,  $\theta_{\mathrm{in}}$  is varied following a Gaussian distribution with a standard deviation of  $5^{\circ}$  to account for the typical etching condition.[51] For statistical average, three simulations are carried out with distinct amorphous cells for each energy-angle combination.

We assume that stable byproducts such as  $\mathrm{SiF}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{SiF}_4$ ,  $\mathrm{N}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $\mathrm{HF}$ ,  $\mathrm{H}_2$ , and  $\mathrm{F}_2$ , easily desorb from the surface owing to the low-pressure conditions of plasma etching. These products that are not bonded to the substrate are identified by analyzing the topology of bond networks and removed after each cycle of molecule injection. 3.2.1. Etching Process at Normal Incidence. We first examine the etching results when HF molecules, with  $E_{\mathrm{in}}$  of 50 eV, strike the surface at normal incidence  $(\theta_{\mathrm{in}} = 0^{\circ})$ . During the etching process, the incident HF molecules undergo collision cascades, leading to three potential outcomes: reflection from the surface, formation of etching products, or implantation within the slab substituting Si-N bonds with Si-F and N-H bonds. Figure 5 illustrates the sequence of surface evolution during etching. Initially, HF molecules break Si-N bonds, modifying the surface layer rather than etching. This modification reduces the atomic density in the surface layers (Figure 5b,c). Once the modified layer reaches a certain thickness, a dynamic equilibrium is established, and the height of the slab begins to decrease (Figure 5d,e).
The variation in the number of each species with respect to the HF dose is depicted in Figure 6a. Notably, the counts of H

The depletion of nitrogen at the surface is consistently observed across a range of incident energies. (See Figure S4). The nitrogen depletion was also noted in several experiments on SiN etching.[54,55] Based on the MD trajectory, we attribute this preferential sputtering of N to the facile formation of  $\mathrm{N}_2$ . This occurs when two N atoms, freed from their bonds due to ion collision, come into proximity and form a stable  $\mathrm{N}_2$  molecule. This newly formed  $\mathrm{N}_2$  may weakly chemisorb or physisorb on the surface, desorbing with minimal energy gain from subsequent ion impacts. Indeed,  $\mathrm{N}_2$  is a common byproduct in various plasma studies.[54-58] We anticipate that plasma etching will reduce the nitrogen content of surfaces, potentially leading to an excess of Si atoms and a degradation in electrical properties.[59,60]

3.2.2. Angular Dependence of Etching Process. Next, we present the results of a-Si $_3$ N $_4$  etching under various angles of incidence, ranging from vertical  $(0^{\circ})$  to nearly horizontal orientations  $(75^{\circ})$ , while  $E_{\mathrm{in}}$  is fixed at 50 eV. Figure 8 depicts the etched structures after a dose of 200 HF/nm² with selected  $\theta_{\mathrm{in}}$ . Notably, as  $\theta_{\mathrm{in}}$  increases, the modified layer becomes progressively thinner. At  $75^{\circ}$ , the modified layer is virtually absent, with the uppermost surface atoms being passivated by H and F atoms (see Figure 8f). To explain this, we plot the reflection coefficients in Figure 9a, which are defined as the ratio of HF molecules reflected without implantation or contributing to etching products. That is to say, incident HF molecules are more likely to be reflected from the surface at high  $\theta_{\mathrm{in}}$ , rather than penetrating into the substrate, which is necessary for developing the modified layer. Consequently, the surface modification is suppressed for high  $\theta_{\mathrm{in}}$  values. Furthermore, the vertical component of the kinetic energy of the incoming HF molecules, which relates to the penetration depth, decreases with  $\theta_{\mathrm{in}}$ . This results in shallower penetration of the molecules, further limiting the growth of the modified layer. To note, it was also observed in experiments that ions show higher probabilities of reflection at increased  $\theta_{\mathrm{in}}$  values.[65,66]

Figure 9b shows the etching yield as a function of  $\theta_{\mathrm{in}}$ . The etching yield decreases rapidly at high  $\theta_{\mathrm{in}}$  values because the reflection coefficient increases and the surface is less modified. It is notable that the etching yield exhibits a peak at  $45^{\circ}$ . This is a result of the interplay of physical sputtering and chemical reaction processes, known as chemical sputtering.[67,68] Chemical sputtering is further discussed in the analysis of desorbed products below.

Figure 10 shows the number of desorbed species at different incident angles. At lower angles, ranging from 0 to  $45^{\circ}$ , the primary products are saturated molecules like  $\mathrm{SiF}_4$ ,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , and  $\mathrm{N}_2$ . This suggests a chemical sputtering process where reactive atoms that have been implanted help in forming the product molecules. Conversely, at higher angles, the product species shifts significantly toward under-coordinated molecules or radicals, such as Si, SiF, N, and NH. This is due to a reduced presence of H and F atoms on the surface at higher angles, which limits the formation of saturated molecules. As a result,

the role of chemical reactions in the overall etching process lessens as surface modifications decrease. A notable finding is the reduced population of  $\mathrm{SiF}_2$  at  $75^{\circ}$ . Although  $\mathrm{SiF}_2$  usually forms easily and desorbs energetically with sufficient surface alteration, it is suppressed because F atoms are depleted at the surface.

The slight increase in etching yield at  $45^{\circ}$  in Figure 9b and the creation of unsaturated radicals in Figure 10 are both results of the combined effects of physical sputtering and chemical reactions. The incoming HF molecules can most efficiently knock out etching byproducts when they strike the surface at oblique angles (physical sputtering). Conversely, reactive atoms are more effectively implanted at shallow angles of incidence, where they interact with the surface to weaken the surface bonds. This exemplifies the dual nature of etching yield observed in chemical sputtering.[67,68] It can be anticipated that higher kinetic energy will yield a stronger peak at the oblique angle due to physical sputtering, as observed in the previous experiment.[69]

3.2.3. Etching Mechanism. Based on the observations on surface species and etching products in the above, we arrive at a schematic etching mechanism as illustrated in Figure 11. At low incident angles, HF molecules can penetrate the  $\mathrm{Si}_3\mathrm{N}_4$  substrate, leading to the formation of modified surface layers. Within these layers, Si-N bonds are progressively replaced by Si-F and N-H bonds. The abundant H and F atoms facilitate the formation of fully coordinated molecules such as  $\mathrm{SiF_4}$  and  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ . Concurrently, a deficiency in N arises due to preferential sputtering. In contrast, at higher incident angles, the implantation of H and F into the surface is reduced. Given

3.3. Bridging to Mesoscale Model. In the previous section, the atomistic MD simulation revealed the finest details of the etching process. While this provides the most accurate description of the process, the size and time scales are far smaller than those required for realistic simulations. To address this, it is necessary to bridge the gap between atomistic simulation and mesoscale models through a coarse-graining procedure. Here, we demonstrate such scale-bridging utilizing our simulation results for the specific case where HF is incident vertically with the kinetic energy of  $50\mathrm{eV}$ .

We have successfully demonstrated the integration of detailed microscale simulation data into a mesoscale model. This bottom-up approach to scale bridging would be useful for accurately predicting the feature profiles in plasma etching, which are significantly influenced by plasma-surface interactions. This methodology would be particularly effective because it allows for the dynamic representation of surface composition changes. As a result, it enhances the modeling precision for advanced plasma etching techniques, such as time-multiplexed processes where the surface composition varies actively over time.
4. CONCLUSION

In summary, we have developed an accurate NNP designed for dry etching of  $\mathrm{a - Si_3N_4}$  using HF molecules. The surface reactions in diverse local environments were sampled by incorporating several types of training set; baseline structures, reaction-specific data, together with general-purpose training sets. Furthermore, the NNP was refined through iterative comparisons with DFT results. Using the trained NNP, we conducted etching simulations with various kinetic energies and incident angles of HF molecules. The etching simulation allowed for detailed observation and analysis of key processes such as preferential sputtering, surface modification, etching yield, threshold energy, and the distribution of etching products. Furthermore, we identified and characterized the formation of a modified surface layer, elucidating its role in chemical sputtering. The observed surface modifications are primarily attributed to preferential sputtering, which involves the generation of  $\mathbf{N}_2$  molecules. We also revealed the angle dependence of the etching mechanism and yield, influenced by variations in reflection coefficients and the development of the modified layer. Finally, we developed a simple mesoscale

model, built from our MD simulation results, which effectively reproduces the surface composition obtained with MD simulations. By establishing a computational framework for atomistic etching simulation and scale bridging, this work paves the way for a more accurate and efficient design of etching processes in the semiconductor industry, thereby enhancing device performance and manufacturing precision.
